project_overview.project_name: Naghlu Hydropower Rehabilitation Project - Phase I
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve dam safety and sustainability of hydropower and to increase the supply of domestically generated hydroelectricity at the Naghlu Hydropower Plant (NHPP)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Electricity users (households, industry, farmers) in Kabul region; DABS (implementing agency); local communities in project area; consumers avoiding diesel self-generation costs

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Surobi District characterized by security problems; project located in high-risk area with escalating insurgency-related violence; Afghanistan overall remains in state of fragility and conflict

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Mechanical, Electrical, and Electromechanical Works
key_components.description: Rehabilitation of Unit 1 and balance of plant; enhancing maintenance of powerhouse including Unit 3 overhaul; provision of spare parts and consumables for 3-5 years; development of warehouses for equipment storage; advisory services for sustainable O&M

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Dam Safety and Power Generation Improvement
key_components.description: Dam safety audit and safety improvement measures including reactivation of bottom outlet, auxiliary power systems, spillway gate improvements, instrumentation installation, UXOS clearance; optimization of power generation through feasibility studies and detailed design for increasing power generation

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Environmental and Social Sustainability, Project Management Support, and Future Project Preparation
key_components.description: Local development assistance including electrification of villages and vocational training; environmental and social management support; project management support; training programs; consulting services; Environmental and Social Advisory Panel (ESAP) and Project Technical Advisory Panel (PTAP) support; future hydropower project preparation

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Da Afghanistan Breshna Sherkat (DABS) - Afghanistan's state-owned power utility
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by deputy minister of finance, including representatives from MEW, agriculture and environment ministries, president's office, and Afghanistan National Security Forces; Project Implementation Team (PIT) within DABS led by project manager reporting to COO; Environmental and Social Advisory Panel (ESAP) and Project Technical Advisory Panel (PTAP) for independent oversight
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: International Competitive Bidding (ICB) for contracts above US$5 million; National Competitive Bidding (NCB) for contracts below US$5 million; Shopping procedures for smaller contracts; World Bank procurement guidelines apply; prior review by Bank for major contracts; procurement plan prepared and agreed with Bank
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: DABS responsible for M&E with support from international consultant; M&E specialist appointed within DABS; quarterly interim financial reports; annual audited financial statements; monitoring framework and baseline indicators established; external monitoring by independent third party; internal audit by DABS Internal Audit Department; external audit by Supreme Audit Office (SAO)

identified_risks.risk_description: High uncertainties in operating environment; escalating insurgency-related violence increasing fragility; possible changes to senior management; government's limited ability to deliver services and programs
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: DABS operation is relatively autonomous and less affected by government changes; Project Steering Committee includes security-related government agencies; benefit-sharing activities to ensure local community support; grievance redress mechanisms

identified_risks.risk_description: Deteriorating security environment could seriously affect project implementation, supervision, and contractor/consultant availability; expected withdrawal of U.S. troops by end of 2016 may worsen security; insecure environment may create difficulties in securing contractors, lead to delays, limit movement for supervision, increase security costs
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of third-party monitoring agent (ARTF Supervisory Agent program) for on-site supervision; benefit-sharing activities with electrification and vocational training to ensure local community support; grievance redress committee for addressing complaints; exploration of IT-based technology for supervision; narrow project scope; contingency planning for unanticipated events

identified_risks.risk_description: Slow economic recovery with real GDP growth at 1.9% in 2015; declining domestic revenue (8.2% of GDP in 2014); high dependence on international donor assistance; fuel subsidies for electricity expected to be phased out by end of 2015
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project provides low-cost domestic generation option; economic analysis shows robust returns even under pessimistic scenarios; project contributes to economic growth and stabilization

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: DABS has weak capacity; low initial capacity results in uncertainties about ability to adapt to good international utility practices; DABS has not implemented Bank-funded project before; overstaffing and under-qualification of staff; poor customer management and billing; tariffs do not fully recover costs
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: International consultants to support and advise DABS staff throughout implementation; capacity building for DABS staff started during preparation and continues during implementation; hiring of project team with necessary technical specialists; training programs and development of operational manuals; mentoring by international procurement specialist and FM specialist; Project Technical Advisory Panel and Environmental and Social Advisory Panel for independent oversight

identified_risks.risk_description: DABS lacks direct experience implementing Bank-funded projects; FM capacity concerns; procurement capacity gaps; understanding of Bank procedures limited; internal audit system gaps; decentralization issues
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: FM specialist hired by DABS; head of Finance Department recently hired with significant Bank experience; accounting manual to be updated; training provided to FM staff on Bank requirements; international procurement specialist hired to build capacity; procurement manual prepared; complaints handling mechanism established; regular FM reviews (two per year); prior review of major contracts; audit arrangements with SAO and private auditors

identified_risks.risk_description: Sector policy uncertainties; need for cost recovery while ensuring affordability; tariff issues; sector knowledge gaps
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project supports sector development through capacity building; alignment with National Energy Supply Program and Power Sector Master Plan; MEW involvement in Project Steering Committee; support for future hydropower project preparation

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: Complex hydropower rehabilitation project; dam safety issues requiring immediate attention; sediment management challenges; presence of unexploded ordnance (UXOS) in reservoir; structural safety concerns under earthquake loading; lack of instrumentation; inoperability of bottom outlet; unavailability of auxiliary spillway gate operation methods
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive dam safety audit to be conducted; Project Technical Advisory Panel to advise on dam safety; dynamic stability analysis to determine structural safety under earthquake loading; detailed studies on structural and operational safety; training of dam staff; installation of essential instrumentation; development of emergency preparedness plans; independent dam safety inspections; consultation with international experts

identified_risks.risk_description: High environmental and social risks including potential loss of assets and land; temporary land acquisition and resettlement in neighboring communities; sediment removal impacts; UXOS handling risks; downstream impacts on aquatic species and irrigation facilities; potential flooding from dam height increase; vulnerable groups affected
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) and Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared and disclosed; separate ESIAs for Subcomponents 2(a) and 2(b); Environmental and Social Advisory Panel for independent oversight; grievance redress mechanism established; community consultations conducted; benefit-sharing programs including electrification and vocational training; pre-feasibility social assessment completed; vulnerable groups prioritized for employment; capacity building for safeguards staff; external monitoring by independent third party

identified_risks.risk_description: Need for local community support in fragile security environment; communities historically felt they did not benefit from dam; coordination with multiple donors (ADB, KfW, USAID); potential for different donor approaches to overwhelm DABS
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Extensive community consultations conducted; Community Development Councils used as primary vehicle for community engagement; benefit-sharing programs (electrification and vocational training) to ensure community support; grievance redress committee established with community representatives; communication and outreach plan to be developed; coordination through ARTF; close contact with other donors to ensure alignment; local development assistance activities

identified_risks.risk_description: During rehabilitation and safety improvement, power production may have to be reduced; Afghanistan's high dependence on energy imports means rehabilitation could be delayed to ensure domestic supply
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong focus on safety from outset of implementation; contingency planning; project design includes phased approach; coordination with DABS on power supply management

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level Grievance Redress Committee (GRC) established in Surobi District with 12 members nominated by communities; comprises community representatives, local government officials, and local NGO representative; DABS developing complaint record database for tracking; GRM training manual being developed; public awareness programs to inform communities about grievance services; three-level mechanism: local resolution, GRC, and appeal to DABS management; World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) also available for project-affected communities
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Women's views captured in social survey through hiring and training of local women; vocational training program includes special plans to target women skills development; women from local area hired to conduct surveys; approximately 20% of vocational training beneficiaries targeted to be women; women's CDCs consulted during project preparation
vulnerable_groups: Low-income consumers currently using diesel self-generation
Communities in project area (Surobi and Tagab Districts) historically not benefiting from dam
Affected persons subject to land acquisition or resettlement

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity in mind. Key measures include: (1) Community benefit-sharing programs (electrification and vocational training) to ensure local support and counter grievances that communities have not benefited from dam; (2) Extensive community consultations using Community Development Councils as primary engagement vehicle; (3) Grievance redress mechanisms at project and Bank levels; (4) Local development assistance to improve community livelihoods; (5) Project Steering Committee includes security-related government agencies for coordination on security issues; (6) Lessons learned from other Afghanistan projects emphasize that projects can only succeed if local communities are convinced of benefits; (7) Narrow project scope to manage complexity in insecure environment; (8) Contingency planning for security-related disruptions
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan remains one of least-developed countries with state of fragility and conflict undermining economic prospects and social cohesion. Key fragility factors: (1) Escalating insurgency-related violence increasing fragility; (2) Lack of security perceived as most important constraint for public service delivery; (3) Insecurity affecting growth and poverty by damaging human capital, constraining productive activities, increasing social unrest, promoting unequal access to services, increasing political instability; (4) Poverty incidence at 35.8% (2011-12); bottom 40% commands only 21% of consumption; over 50% risk descending into poverty; (5) Slow economic recovery with real GDP growth at 1.9% in 2015; (6) Declining domestic revenue (8.2% of GDP in 2014); (7) Expected withdrawal of U.S. troops by end of 2016 may worsen security; (8) Project area (Surobi District) characterized by security problems; (9) Decades of civil war and insurgency have devastated energy sector infrastructure (65% of transmission lines destroyed); (10) Government capacity constraints in service delivery
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security is major implementation risk. Specific considerations: (1) Deteriorating security environment could affect project implementation, supervision, and contractor/consultant availability; (2) Limited number of contractors and consultants willing to work in sensitive areas; (3) Difficulties in securing and maintaining contracting firms; (4) Potential early termination or abandonment of assignments; (5) Delays in rehabilitation; (6) Limitations on Bank staff movement for field supervision; (7) Logistical problems for supervision; (8) Increased demand for additional financial resources for security needs; (9) Surobi District is high-risk area; (10) Mitigation through third-party monitoring agent (ARTF Supervisory Agent program) for on-site supervision; (11) Use of IT-based technology for supervision; (12) Benefit-sharing activities to maintain community support; (13) Inclusion of security agencies in Project Steering Committee; (14) Contingency planning for unanticipated security events
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Potential displacement and resettlement issues identified. Details: (1) Involuntary Resettlement Policy (OP 4.12) triggered due to possible land acquisition, asset loss, and resettlement in connection with Subcomponents 2(a) and 2(b); (2) Sediment removal from reservoir may result in permanent asset loss and temporary land acquisition; (3) Decision to increase storage capacity of dam could result in unavoidable resettlement of people, disruption of community life and networks, permanent or temporary loss of land, assets, livelihoods, and communal/social/cultural facilities (cemeteries, shrines, mosques, grazing land); (4) Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) developed as part of ESMF and will be applied to all project components where land acquisition, asset loss, and/or resettlement cannot be avoided; (5) Separate ESIAs will be carried out for Subcomponents 2(a) and 2(b) to inform development of ESMPs and Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) if required; (6) RAPs will comply with resettlement principles regarding eligibility criteria, compensation categories and rates, and organizational arrangements; (7) Pre-feasibility social assessment conducted in 64 villages (28 villages within 3 km of dam, 36 additional villages 5-25 km away); (8) Subcomponent 3(a) includes benefit-sharing through electrification and vocational training for affected communities; (9) Very small areas of land may be acquired on willing buyer-willing seller basis for electricity poles and pylons; (10) Grievance redress mechanisms in place for affected persons
